name: inventory
context: Inspect, validate, and target Ansible inventory so automation runs against the correct hosts
info: Inventory is the execution boundary for Ansible: it declares managed hosts, groups, connection variables, and environment-specific data that every ad-hoc command and playbook resolves before it runs. This skill focuses on the practical inventory jobs agents perform repeatedly: list resolved hosts, graph group relationships, inspect host variables, work with multiple inventory sources, separate environments to reduce blast radius, and confirm targeting before running disruptive automation. It uses `ansible-inventory` and the inventory guide rather than guessing from raw files, which matters whenever host/group vars, plugins, or multiple sources change what Ansible actually sees.
triggers: show me the resolved ansible inventory
check what hosts ansible will target
body:
# Ansible Inventory

Inventory tells Ansible what exists, how to connect, and what data is attached to each host or group. If you skip inventory validation, every later step is built on an assumption that may be wrong. Before running [[ansible/ad-hoc]] or [[ansible/playbooks]], inspect the resolved inventory exactly as Ansible sees it.

## Static Inventory Example

```yaml
all:
  children:
    web:
      hosts:
        app1.example.com:
        app2.example.com:
      vars:
        ansible_user: deploy
    db:
      hosts:
        db1.example.com:
          ansible_host: 10.0.10.21
          ansible_python_interpreter: /usr/bin/python3
```

## Inspect the Resolved Result

```bash
ansible-inventory -i inventory.yml --list
ansible-inventory -i inventory.yml --graph
ansible-inventory -i inventory.yml --host app1.example.com
```

## Typical Layout

```text
inventory/
  hosts.yml
  group_vars/
    all.yml
    web.yml
  host_vars/
    app1.example.com.yml
```

## Multiple Sources and Dynamic Inventory

Ansible can merge multiple `-i` sources and inventory plugins. Prefer plugins over legacy scripts for cloud, CMDB, or API-backed inventory. Keep separate inventories for environments such as `test`, `staging`, and `prod` so it is harder to change the wrong fleet accidentally.

## Targeting with Patterns

Patterns are how later commands choose hosts from inventory: `all`, `web`, `db:&prod`, `web:!canary`, or a hostname directly. Validate the set before execution:

```bash
ansible-inventory -i inventory.yml --graph
ansible web -i inventory.yml -m ansible.builtin.ping --limit canary
```

sections.title: Static files, directories, and plugins
sections.body: Inventory can be a single file, a directory of files, or one or more plugin-backed sources. Static YAML inventory is easier to review in Git. Directories scale better because `group_vars/` and `host_vars/` can live next to the source. Dynamic inventory plugins are preferred over legacy scripts for cloud and CMDB-backed sources. Use separate sources per environment when blast radius matters.

sections.title: Use ansible-inventory instead of guessing
sections.body: Run `ansible-inventory -i SOURCE --list` for the resolved machine-readable inventory, `--graph` for group structure, and `--host NAME` for the final variables on one host. This exposes inheritance, plugin output, and file merges that are not obvious from raw YAML or INI files. Load this node whenever targeting is in doubt.

sections.title: Patterns decide which hosts execute
sections.body: Patterns such as `web`, `db:&prod`, and `web:!canary` are the selector language used by both [[ansible/ad-hoc]] and [[ansible/playbooks]]. Validate patterns against inventory before doing anything destructive. If a playbook uses `-l` or `--limit`, that limit is applied after inventory resolution and can save you from a full-fleet mistake.

sections.title: host_vars and group_vars layout
sections.body: Keep shared defaults in `group_vars/all.yml`, role or tier data in group files such as `group_vars/web.yml`, and machine-specific settings in `host_vars/<hostname>.yml`. Inventory variables are high-impact because they often control connection user, interpreter path, become behavior, and service endpoints. See [[ansible/config]] for how these values interact with CLI flags and extra vars.
